title: Collaborative Editing (Google Docs) — System Design
description: Design real-time collaborative document editing: many users edit the same document simultaneously and everyone converges on the same result. The themes are conflict resolution (OT vs CRDT), an authoritative ordered operation stream, presence, and durability via snapshots + op log.

nodes.label: Editor Client
nodes.description: Edits the document locally and syncs ops; renders other users' changes and cursors.

nodes.label: Connection Gateway
nodes.description: Routes WebSocket connections to the doc server owning a document.

nodes.label: Document Collab Server
nodes.description: Authoritative per-document session: orders ops, broadcasts, holds live state.

nodes.label: Operation Log
nodes.description: Durable ordered log of operations per document (the source of truth).

nodes.label: Snapshot Store
nodes.description: Periodic materialized document snapshots to bound replay.

nodes.label: In-Memory Presence
nodes.description: Live cursors, selections, and who-is-editing per document — ephemeral in-memory state on the owning doc server, not a durable store. A dedicated pub/sub tier is an alternative for cross-doc aggregation.

nodes.label: Persistence Worker
nodes.description: Compacts the op log into snapshots and archives history.

nodes.label: Document Router
nodes.description: Maps document id -> the doc server currently owning it (sharding).

nodes.label: Document Metadata Store
nodes.description: Doc ownership, ACLs, titles, version markers.

nodes.label: Client Local Pending Ops
nodes.description: Client-local (on-device) pending-op buffer — local storage inside the editor app, not a reachable backend queue. Holds optimistic ops (acked or not) and survives reload/restart; replayed to the owning server on reconnect.

functional: Multiple users edit the same document at the same time.
Every user's edits appear to others in real time.
All clients converge on the same state for the same accepted operation set under the server's ordering/merge rules.
Show presence: live cursors, selections, and who's editing.
Support offline edits that merge on reconnect; keep version history.
nonFunctional: Low-latency local feedback: a user's own keystrokes feel instant (optimistic local apply).
Convergence/correctness: concurrent edits must never diverge or lose intent.
Durable: an acknowledged edit is never lost.
Scale to many documents with bounded concurrent editors per doc.
Available: brief server blips shouldn't lose work (offline buffering).

capacity.label: Peak editors / hot doc
capacity.note: Bounded by product; the hard part is correctness, not editor count. A '50 concurrent' cap is enforced and is why one owning server per doc is sufficient.

capacity.label: Active docs (peak)
capacity.note: Concurrently open editing sessions out of billions of stored docs, sharded across owning servers. Most are NOT hot — see the editors-per-doc distribution below; fleet sizing is based on that distribution, not on 2M docs all at the 50-editor cap.

capacity.label: Editors / typical active doc
capacity.note: The common case: a doc open by one author or a couple of collaborators. Op rate per doc is a handful/sec, fanout is trivial.

capacity.label: Editors / p95 active doc
capacity.note: Busier shared docs (a team doc in a meeting). Still far below the hot-doc cap; one owner server handles many of these concurrently.

capacity.label: Ops/editor/sec while typing
capacity.note: Keystrokes batched (debounced ~50–100ms) into ops; ~5 ops/s is a typical sustained typing rate after batching.

capacity.label: Op size
capacity.note: Insert/delete + position/id + client metadata (clientOpId, baseVersion, session). Small but not free at fanout scale.

capacity.label: Edit fanout / hot doc
capacity.note: accepted ops/s × connected editors = (5 ops/s × 50 editors) × 50 editors = 12.5K broadcast messages/s on a full hot doc. This is the per-owner-server hot path.

capacity.label: Presence updates/editor/sec
capacity.note: Cursor/selection moves are far more frequent than edits; throttled to ~10/s. Presence fanout on a hot doc ≈ 10 × 50 × 50 = 25K msg/s — which is why it bypasses the durable op log.

capacity.label: Op-log write rate / hot doc
capacity.note: 5 ops/s × 50 editors. Durable append-before-ack must keep up with this per owning doc server.

capacity.label: Op throughput / hot doc
capacity.note: Worst case only: 250 ops/s × 100 bytes. A typical 1–3-editor doc is a tiny fraction of this. Sustained 24h at the hot rate would be ~2 GB/day before overhead — but no doc is edited flat-out at the cap all day.

capacity.label: Storage / hot doc / active hour
capacity.note: 25 KB/s × 3600s, hot-doc worst case, before snapshot/index/retention overhead. Real docs accrue far less: editing happens in bursts of minutes, not sustained hours, and most docs are not hot. Snapshots + op compaction bound long-term growth; archived history is kept for version browsing.

capacity.label: Snapshot cadence
capacity.note: Tuning knob: bounds the tail to replay on attach/failover. ~1–5K ops keeps reload < ~1s while not snapshotting wastefully.

capacity.label: Acceptable broadcast latency
capacity.note: Remote edits should appear near-instantly; local edits are already optimistic so they feel instant regardless.

api.description: Attach to a document's live session; authorize, then receive current state (snapshot + tail ops), head version, a session identity, and the current owner epoch.
api.request: Upgrade: websocket (doc id, auth token, lastSeenVersion?)

api.description: Client sends an edit op against the version it last saw, tagged with a durable clientOpId (UUID) plus a per-session monotonic clientSeq so the server can dedupe retries. Before appending, the server re-checks the caller's ACL (via aclVersion) and that it still holds the active owner epoch for the doc — a revoked editor or a stale owner is rejected, not appended. Server transforms, orders, durably appends, and acks with the assigned version. Resending the same clientOpId after a lost ack returns the original ack without reapplying. The owner epoch is attached server-side at append time after routing, so the client never sends it.

Best-effort cursor/selection update multiplexed on the same WebSocket. Throttled (~10/s), lossy, never persisted to the op log. Carries the document version it was anchored to so the server can transform it against ops that landed since.
api.response: (no ack — fire-and-forget; server fans out others' cursors on the same channel)

api.description: Resume a session after a disconnect (network flap or offline gap, including a full app restart). The client persists its sessionId alongside the pending-op queue, so it reconnects under the same sessionId and resends pending ops with their original clientOpId/clientSeq — that durable identity is what lets the server dedupe even after a multi-day gap. Server returns the missed ops or asks for a full snapshot resync, then accepts the pending ops idempotently.

messages.label: dedupe pending by clientOpId; transform vs 413..460

dataModel.name: operations
dataModel.note: Append-only ordered op log per document — the source of truth. The canonical dedupe key is client-op-scoped: (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id) is unique, so a retry is idempotent even if the editing session was regenerated (reload/restart/long offline gap). (session_id, client_seq) is also stored to reconcile a client's pending queue in order. (doc_id, version) is monotonic under the active owner epoch.

fields.name: owner_epoch
fields.type: bigint (fences appends from a stale owner)

fields.name: op
fields.type: json OT op: { type, pos|range, text|attributes }

fields.name: client_op_id
fields.type: uuid (durable per-op id; survives session change)

fields.name: session_id
fields.type: string (editing session; persisted with pending queue)

fields.name: client_seq
fields.type: bigint (monotonic per session; orders the pending queue)

fields.name: op_schema_version
fields.type: smallint (op format/migration; e.g. v1 text, v2 attributes/ranges, v3 comments)

dataModel.name: snapshots
dataModel.note: Periodic materialized document state to bound replay. Written atomically; a checksum guards against a half-written snapshot after a worker crash.

fields.name: version
fields.type: bigint (snapshot is state up to this version)

fields.name: checksum
fields.type: string (validated on load; reject partial writes)

dataModel.name: documents
dataModel.note: Ownership lease, ACLs, and current version markers. owner_epoch + lease_expires_at fence ownership so only one active writer can stamp versions.

fields.name: lease_expires_at
fields.type: timestamp (owner must renew or lose the lease)

fields.name: acl_version
fields.type: bigint (bumped on permission change; checked on op)

patterns.name: Optimistic local apply
patterns.what: Apply the user's own edit locally immediately, then reconcile with the server.
patterns.whenToUse: Editing UIs where input latency must feel instant.

patterns.name: Single authoritative ordering
patterns.what: One server per document assigns a total order to ops; everyone replays that order.
patterns.whenToUse: When all replicas must converge deterministically.

patterns.name: Operational Transformation (OT)
patterns.what: Transform a concurrent op against ops it didn't see so positions stay correct.

patterns.name: CRDT
patterns.what: Represent the document so concurrent ops commute and merge without a central transform.

patterns.name: Snapshot + op log
patterns.what: Persist an append-only op log and periodic snapshots to bound replay and recover.

patterns.name: Offline buffering + replay
patterns.what: Queue ops made offline and merge them (via OT/CRDT) on reconnect.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Last-Write-Wins Whole-Document Save
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Each client edits its own copy and periodically saves the whole document back with PUT /doc/{id}; the server stores whatever blob it last received. To see others' changes, a client reloads the document.

This works for a single editor and immediately falls apart with two. Because each save overwrites the entire document, the last writer wins: if Alice and Bob both load v5, Alice saves her paragraph, then Bob saves his (built on the stale v5), Bob's save clobbers Alice's work entirely — the classic lost-update problem. There's no merge, no notion of concurrent edits, and edits feel laggy because nothing appears until a save+reload round trip.

So the baseline motivates the whole design: apply edits optimistically and locally for instant feedback, express them as fine-grained operations against a known baseline version, give one authority a total order, and reconcile concurrent ops with OT or a CRDT so no edit is ever silently lost.

(The API evolves with the design: this baseline's blob-style PUT /doc/{id} is replaced by the WebSocket op stream — connect, send-op, reconnect — once edits become fine-grained operations, so the API section lists the op-stream contract, not the discarded PUT.)

view.caption: The editor client connects over WSS through the connection gateway to a document collab server, which appends each op to the operation log.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two people load the same document, both edit, and both save. With whole-document last-write-wins, what happens to the first person's changes?
whyNow: Establishing last-write-wins first makes the lost-update problem concrete — every later step (optimistic ops, single ordering, OT/CRDT) exists to stop one editor from silently clobbering another.

recap.after: Clients save the whole document; the last save wins; others reload to see changes.
recap.newRisk: Concurrent edits clobber each other (lost updates), there's no merge, and editing feels laggy because nothing is real-time.

traps.trap: Saving and overwriting the whole document on each edit.
traps.why: A whole-document write built on a stale copy silently discards everyone else's concurrent edits — the lost-update problem.
traps.instead: Send fine-grained ops against a baseline version, order them centrally, and merge concurrent ops via OT/CRDT.

steps.title: 2. Optimistic Local Editing
description: Typing must feel instant, so the client applies its own edits to its local copy immediately rather than waiting for a server round trip. Each edit is an operation (insert/delete at a position) sent to the server in the background.
The client tracks the version it last synced from the server; ops it sends are relative to that baseline. The local copy is provisional until the server orders and acks.

Concretely the client keeps a pending-op queue: the acked server version plus the ops it has sent but not yet had acked. Each op gets a monotonic clientSeq (which orders the queue) and a durable, client-generated clientOpId (a UUID — the dedupe key the server keys on). The queue is persisted to local storage before an op is considered locally accepted, so it survives a tab reload, app restart, or a multi-day offline gap; on resend, the clientOpId is unchanged, so the server recognizes the op as a duplicate even if the editing session was regenerated.

When a remote op arrives before its own pending ops are acked, the client rebases its pending ops over that remote op (the same transform machinery it uses on the server). This persisted pending queue is exactly what makes later retry, dedupe, and offline replay work — a lost ack just means resending ops under their original clientOpId.
This optimistic model is what makes collaborative editors usable — but it means clients can produce edits concurrently against the same baseline, which must be reconciled.

view.caption: The editor client connects through the gateway to the collab server and sends ops, applying them locally first for an instant edit.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why apply the user's keystroke locally before the server confirms it?

concepts.definition: Apply local edits immediately, reconcile with the server asynchronously.
concepts.whyItMatters: Removes input latency; the network round trip never blocks typing.
concepts.example: You see your character instantly; the op syncs in the background.

concepts.definition: The ordered list of sent-but-unacked ops the client holds, rebasing them over any remote op that arrives before they're acked.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the substrate for retry, dedupe, and offline replay — a lost ack just means resending the queue.
concepts.example: Pending [seq 87, 88]; a remote insert lands, so both are transformed before re-sending.

concepts.definition: The pending queue (with its sessionId) is persisted to local storage before an op is locally accepted, and each op carries a client-generated clientOpId (UUID).
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes dedupe survive reloads, app restarts, and multi-day offline gaps — the buffered op resubmits under the same identity instead of evading the dedupe key as a 'new' op.
concepts.example: Reload after editing offline; the same clientOpId d4e1-… resubmits and the server returns the original ack.

whyNow: Local optimism is the UX foundation and the source of concurrency; everything about conflict resolution exists to reconcile it.

recap.after: Instant local editing; ops streamed to the server.
recap.newRisk: Two users editing the same baseline concurrently produce conflicting ops — someone must order and reconcile them.

steps.title: 3. One Authoritative Order per Document
description: Convergence requires a single source of truth for order. Assign each active document to one collab server that receives all ops for it, assigns a monotonic version number, appends to the op log, and broadcasts to all editors.
Every client replays ops in this server-assigned order, so all replicas converge. The op log is durable and authoritative; the live in-memory document is derived from it.
A document router maps doc id -> owning server (sharding by doc). Bounded editors-per-doc means one server per doc is fine; documents scale horizontally across servers.

Single-owner ordering is only correct if there is exactly one active writer. The dangerous case is an old owner that pauses (GC pause, network partition) rather than dying, then resumes after the router has handed the doc to a new owner — now two servers could both stamp versions. The fix is fencing: each ownership is an epoch (a lease). The owner includes its owner_epoch in every op-log append, and the log rejects any append from a stale epoch. A resumed old owner's writes are fenced out, so the (doc_id, version) sequence stays monotonic under exactly one epoch.

Lease mechanics in practice: the owner holds a short lease (say ~10s TTL) and renews it roughly every TTL/3 (~3s) against the lease store. Failure detection waits for the lease to expire, so failover time is bounded by the TTL (~10s here) rather than being instantaneous. The new owner only takes the bumped epoch after the old lease has provably expired, so the active windows of the two owners never overlap. A 'false failover' — the owner was merely slow, not dead — is harmless: fencing rejects the slow owner's stale-epoch appends instead of corrupting order, and it just reconnects. The TTL is the knob: short enough to fail over quickly, long enough not to flap on a normal GC pause.

view.caption: The document router consults the metadata store for ownership and ACL, then the single owning collab server serializes ops into the operation log and broadcasts them.

options.name: Single owning server per document (default)
options.description: Each active document is routed to one collab server that assigns a monotonic version to every op, appends it to the durable op log, and broadcasts to editors.
pros: One server assigns the monotonic version and broadcasts, so every client replays the same order and replicas converge.
Trivial total order: no consensus round per op, just an in-memory sequence appended to the op log.
Documents shard cleanly across servers (router maps doc id -> owner), so the system scales by document.
cons: The owning server is a per-document single point of failure; a crash needs failover and reload from snapshot+tail.
All ops for a hot document funnel through one server's throughput.
Router/ownership state must stay correct or two servers could both think they own a doc — addressed by leasing ownership with a monotonic epoch and fencing stale-epoch appends at the op log.

view.caption: The client routes through the document router and metadata store to the one server that owns the doc, which appends ordered ops to the log and broadcasts them.

options.name: Consensus log across replicas (Raft/Paxos)
options.description: A replicated log elects a leader that sequences ops; the order is committed to a quorum of followers before the op is acknowledged.
pros: No single owning server: a replicated log elects a leader and survives a node loss without manual failover.
The committed op order is durable on a quorum before it is acked.
Natural fit if you already run a consensus-backed store for the op log.
cons: A consensus round-trip per batch of ops adds latency the optimistic-typing UX is trying to hide.
Far more machinery (election, log replication) than one doc usually needs given bounded editors-per-doc.
Leader is still a write funnel per document — you don't escape the single-orderer bottleneck, you just make it HA.

view.caption: A Raft leader for the doc replicates each op to followers A and B and commits to the operation log only on quorum.

options.name: Leaderless / peer-to-peer (no central order)
options.description: Clients exchange ops directly and merge them with commutative CRDT operations, so replicas converge without any node assigning a central order.
pros: No owning server to fail; clients exchange ops directly and merge with commutative (CRDT) operations.
Works offline-first and for true peer-to-peer editing with no always-on backend.
Eliminates the per-document throughput funnel entirely.
cons: Requires CRDTs (commutative ops) — you can't bolt this onto positional OT that needs a defined order.
No authoritative version number for snapshots, history browsing, or access control checkpoints.
Per-character metadata and tombstones grow, and a durable op log still needs some coordinator to persist to.

view.caption: Editor peers A, B, and C gossip ops directly to each other with no central server imposing an order.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why route all edits for one document through a single server instead of any server?

concepts.definition: One owner per document totally orders its ops.
concepts.whyItMatters: A single authoritative sequence is the simplest way to guarantee all replicas converge.
concepts.example: Doc 9 -> server S3; S3 stamps versions 412, 413, ... and everyone replays in that order.

concepts.definition: Ownership is a leased epoch; the owner stamps owner_epoch on every append and the op log rejects appends from a stale epoch.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents split-brain: a paused old owner that resumes after failover cannot corrupt the version sequence, so single-owner ordering stays correct.
concepts.example: Router bumps epoch 9 -> 10 on transfer; old owner's epoch-9 append is rejected.

whyNow: A single ordering authority per doc makes convergence tractable; it's the structural decision the conflict algorithm builds on.

recap.after: One server totally orders each doc's ops under a fenced ownership epoch; clients replay it.
recap.newRisk: An op was written against an old baseline — its position may be wrong relative to ops that landed first.

traps.trap: Treating ownership transfer as just a router table update with no fencing.
traps.why: A paused old owner can resume after the router reassigns the doc and both servers stamp versions — split brain forks the authoritative order, the one invariant the whole design rests on.
traps.instead: Lease ownership with a monotonic owner_epoch; stamp it on every append; have the op log reject stale-epoch writes.

flows.name: Optimistic op -> single-server ordering -> broadcast
flows.note: The client applies its op locally at once, then the owning server assigns the next version, appends to the durable log, and broadcasts the ordered op to all editors.

flows.name: Ownership failover with fencing (no split brain)
flows.note: When an owner is suspected dead, the router transfers the lease by bumping the epoch. The new owner fences the old one, loads snapshot + tail, and accepts reconnects. If the old owner was only paused and resumes, its stale-epoch appends are rejected by the op log.

messages.label: old owner was only paused and resumes

failureDrills.scenario: The old owner pauses (long GC / network partition), the router assigns a new owner, then the old owner resumes and tries to append.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The old owner's appends carry a stale owner_epoch and are rejected by the op log; only the new owner (current epoch) can stamp versions, so the (doc_id, version) sequence never forks.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lease + monotonic owner_epoch fencing; epoch stamped on every append; op log enforces current-epoch-only writes.

steps.title: 4. Conflict Resolution: OT vs CRDT
description: A client's op targets a position in the version it saw; by the time it reaches the server, other ops may have shifted that position. Naively applying it corrupts the text. Two families solve this.

Operational Transformation (OT): the server transforms an incoming op against the concurrent ops it didn't see, adjusting positions so intent is preserved — the classic Google-Docs approach, well-suited to a central server.

And it's not just shifting insert positions. The harder pairs are where the difficulty lives: in a delete-vs-insert, if a concurrent delete removed the range your insert was anchored inside, the insert has to reanchor to where that text went (clamp to the deletion point) rather than land among characters that no longer exist. In a delete-vs-delete, two clients deleting overlapping ranges must transform to remove only the characters still present — e.g. you meant to delete [5,10) but a concurrent [3,8) already landed, so the overlap [5,8) is gone and your op becomes deleting just the remaining [8,10) at its shifted position. Each such operation pair is a transform function you have to prove correct, which is exactly why OT is 'simple centrally but the transforms are tricky'.

CRDTs: represent the document so operations commute by design (each character gets a stable, orderable id, not a positional index), so concurrent edits merge without a central transform — better for peer-to-peer/offline. The mirror-image cost is that CRDTs converge easily but carry per-character metadata and tombstone overhead. Pick one and own its cost.

For this server-centric, Google-Docs-style case the target is OT: we already have a single owning server defining order, so transforming against concurrent ops is the natural fit and keeps the document a compact character sequence. CRDT stays the alternative for an offline-first/P2P product (and a follow-up). One caveat to name out loud: rich text (bold, links, comments) multiplies the OT transform cases — every new operation type needs its transform proven against every other — which is why formatting is left as a deliberate extension here.

view.caption: The collab server receives client ops, appends them to the operation log, and broadcasts transformed ops back to the client.

options.name: Operational Transformation (default)
options.description: The central server transforms an incoming op against the concurrent ops it didn't see, shifting its positions so the original edit intent is preserved.
pros: Keeps the document as a plain character sequence — small per-op metadata, no per-character tombstones.
Fits the single-owning-server model already chosen: the server defines order and transforms incoming ops.
Battle-tested for central-server text editing (the classic Google Docs approach).
cons: Transform functions must be correct for every op-pair; getting them right is notoriously hard.
Effectively requires a central server to define concurrency — weak fit for peer-to-peer/offline-first.
Adding new operation types means new transform cases to prove correct.

view.caption: The collab server transforms each incoming op against concurrent ops before appending to the log and broadcasting the transformed result.

options.name: CRDT (commutative ops)
options.description: Each character carries a stable, globally-orderable id instead of a positional index, so concurrent inserts and deletes commute and merge without a central transform.
pros: Each character gets a stable, globally-orderable id so concurrent inserts/deletes merge by construction — no central transform.
Shines for offline-heavy and peer-to-peer editing where there is no single arbiter of order.
Convergence is a property of the data type, so there are no fragile per-op-pair transform functions.
cons: Per-character metadata and tombstones for deletes inflate document size and memory.
Garbage-collecting tombstones safely is its own hard problem.
Heavier than OT for the common case of a few editors on a central server.

view.caption: The CRDT collab server appends commutative ops to the log and broadcasts them, letting clients converge without transformation.

options.name: Locking / pessimistic regions
options.description: A client acquires a lock on a paragraph or region before editing it, so no two editors ever touch the same span concurrently and there is nothing to merge.
pros: Conceptually simple: a client holds a lock on a paragraph/region and no one else can edit it concurrently.
No transform or merge logic at all — there are never concurrent edits to reconcile.
Easy to reason about correctness.
cons: Destroys the real-time feel: editors block waiting for locks instead of typing freely.
Lock granularity is a lose-lose: too coarse blocks collaboration, too fine is chatty and complex.
A client that holds a lock and disconnects strands the region until a timeout.

view.caption: The collab server acts as a lock manager, acquiring a region lock from the lock table before applying an edit.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two users insert at position 5 simultaneously. How do you keep both edits and converge?

concepts.definition: Transform a concurrent op against unseen ops so its effect is correct in the new order.
concepts.whyItMatters: Preserves edit intent under concurrency with a central authority.
concepts.example: Op 'insert @5' after a prior 'insert @3' is transformed to 'insert @6'.

concepts.definition: A data structure whose concurrent operations commute, so replicas converge without coordination.
concepts.whyItMatters: Convergence without a central transform; great for offline/P2P.
concepts.example: Each char has a unique fractional id; inserts are ordered by id, not index.

whyNow: Conflict resolution is the intellectual core of the case; articulating OT vs CRDT and their tradeoff is exactly the senior signal.

recap.after: Concurrent edits converge via OT (the target); CRDT is the offline-first/P2P alternative.
recap.newRisk: The op log grows forever — replaying from scratch gets slow; state must persist durably.

traps.trap: Applying a client's op at its literal position without transforming.
traps.why: Concurrent edits shift positions; literal application corrupts the document and diverges replicas.
traps.instead: Transform (OT) against concurrent ops, or use a CRDT whose ops commute.

flows.name: Transform a stale-baseline op against concurrent ops (OT)
flows.note: An op written against an old version is transformed against the ops that landed since, so its effect is correct in the new order; a CRDT reaches the same convergence by making ops commute by construction.

steps.title: 4a. Choosing Between OT and CRDT
description: Both families converge concurrent edits, but the choice is a real fork with different costs. Operational Transformation keeps the document as a plain sequence and transforms each op against the ops it didn't see; it leans on a central server to define the order, and the transform functions are notoriously tricky to get right for every op-pair.

CRDTs give each character a stable, globally-orderable id so concurrent inserts/deletes commute by construction — no central transform, which is why they shine for peer-to-peer and offline-heavy editing. The cost is per-character metadata (tombstones for deletes, id overhead) that has to be managed.
Rule of thumb: a server-centric editor (Google Docs) does well with OT; an offline-first or P2P editor (many modern local-first apps) leans CRDT. State the assumption, pick one, and own its cost.

Target choice for this case: OT. We have a single owning server per document (the ordering step), bounded editors, and no requirement for true peer-to-peer offline merge without a server — so OT's compact state and central-transform model wins, and we accept the cost of carefully-proven transform functions. CRDT is the alternative we'd reach for if the product became offline-first or P2P.

view.caption: The collab server appends ordered ops to the operation log, the shared substrate both OT and CRDT build on.

steps.decisionPrompt: Server-centric vs offline-first: which conflict model do you choose, and what's the cost you accept?

concepts.definition: OT = central-order transforms (compact state, tricky transforms); CRDT = commutative ops via per-element ids (simple convergence, metadata overhead).
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the defining algorithm decision; the right pick depends on whether you have a central authority and how offline-heavy you are.
concepts.example: Google Docs uses OT; local-first/P2P editors use CRDTs.

traps.trap: Hand-waving 'just use a CRDT' (or OT) without naming the cost.
traps.why: OT transform functions are hard to make correct; CRDTs carry unbounded metadata if tombstones aren't pruned.
traps.instead: Tie the choice to the topology (central vs P2P/offline) and acknowledge the cost.

recap.before: Concurrent edits converge by some mechanism.
recap.after: Target committed: OT for this server-centric editor; CRDT kept as the offline-first/P2P alternative.
recap.newRisk: OT transform correctness — every op-pair (and every future rich-text op type) must transform correctly.

steps.title: 5. Durability: Snapshots + Op Log
description: The op log is the source of truth, but replaying millions of ops to load a document is too slow. Periodically materialize a snapshot of the document at a version; loading = latest snapshot + the tail of ops after it.

A persistence worker compacts the log into snapshots and archives history for version browsing. Acknowledge an op only after it's durably in the log, so an acked edit is never lost even if the server crashes. Persist-before-ack is also what makes retries safe: because the durable append is keyed by (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id), a client that retries after a lost ack — even from a fresh session after a reload — hits the unique constraint and the server returns the original version instead of applying the op twice.

Snapshots must be written consistently: a snapshot is the materialized state up to an exact version, written atomically with a checksum and a format_version. If the worker crashes mid-write, the partial blob fails its checksum on load and the reader falls back to the previous snapshot + a longer tail — a half-written snapshot is never trusted. The documents row's snapshot_version is only advanced after the snapshot is durably committed.

Retention is a real constraint here because version history is a requirement: compaction can prune the op-log tail a recent snapshot already covers, but it cannot simply discard all old ops. So history lives in cold storage as a chain of periodic archived snapshots plus the op stream between adjacent restore points. 'Browse history' lists the archived snapshots; 'restore to version V' loads the nearest archived snapshot at or before V and replays the cold ops up to V — the same snapshot+tail mechanism, just over cold storage rather than the live op log, which after hot compaction no longer holds the old ops.
On crash/failover, a new server for the doc reloads from snapshot + tail and resumes the session — the in-memory state is rebuildable.

view.caption: The collab server appends ops to the log and loads from the snapshot store on attach, while a persistence worker reads the log and writes periodic snapshots.

options.name: Snapshot + op-log tail (default)
options.description: A persistence worker periodically materializes a document snapshot at a version; loading reads the latest snapshot plus the short tail of ops appended after it.
pros: Loading is a recent snapshot plus the short tail of ops after it — fast even for a doc with millions of historical ops.
The op log stays the durable source of truth, so an acked edit survives a crash.
Crash/failover just reloads snapshot + tail and resumes; in-memory state is fully rebuildable.
cons: A background persistence worker must compact the log into snapshots and archive history.
Snapshot cadence is a tuning knob: too rare means a long tail replay, too frequent wastes work.
Two artifacts (snapshot store + op log) to keep consistent and version-aligned.

view.caption: The persistence worker periodically snapshots the document and the server replays only the op-log tail past the latest snapshot on attach.

options.name: Full op-log replay (no snapshots)
options.description: The append-only op log is the entire persistence story; loading a document replays every op from version zero with no snapshots to short-circuit it.
pros: Simplest possible model: one durable, append-only log is the entire persistence story.
No snapshot worker, no snapshot/op-log consistency to manage.
Complete, replayable history is always available for auditing or time-travel.
cons: Opening a long-lived document replays millions of ops — load time grows without bound.
Crash recovery is slow for the same reason: rebuild from op zero.
Log storage grows forever with no compaction.

view.caption: The collab server keeps only the full operation log and replays every op from the start to rebuild the document.

options.name: Materialized document blob (overwrite)
options.description: The persistence worker overwrites a single stored blob with the current document state, so loading is one read and there is no op log to replay.
pros: Loading is a single read of the current document state — no replay at all.
Storage is bounded to roughly one document size, not full history.
Easy to reason about: the store holds exactly the latest version.
cons: No op log means no fine-grained crash recovery — an in-flight edit not yet flushed is lost.
Loses edit history, so version browsing and audit are gone.
Persisting the whole blob frequently is expensive for large documents.

view.caption: The persistence worker overwrites a single latest-document blob that the collab server loads whole on attach.

steps.decisionPrompt: A document has 2 million historical ops. How do you load it quickly and recover after a crash?

concepts.definition: Load the latest snapshot plus only the ops after it, instead of replaying the whole log.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds load/recovery time regardless of document age.

concepts.definition: Durably appending keyed by (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id) so a retried op is recognized and not applied twice — even after a reload regenerates the session.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reconnects, lost acks, tab reloads, and offline replay all resend ops; without idempotency the same insert lands twice — a correctness bug.
concepts.example: Retry of clientOpId d4e1-… returns the original v413 instead of appending v461.

concepts.definition: A snapshot is the state up to an exact version, written atomically with a checksum; a partial write is rejected on load.
concepts.whyItMatters: A half-written snapshot adopted as truth would silently corrupt the document on the next failover.
concepts.example: Worker dies mid-write; checksum fails; reader uses the prior snapshot + a longer tail.

whyNow: Durability + bounded replay is what makes the authoritative-log model practical and crash-safe; it's the reliability backbone.

recap.before: Replay the whole log to load.
recap.after: Snapshot+tail load; acked ops durable; sessions rebuildable.
recap.newRisk: Editors disconnect and edit offline — those ops must merge cleanly on return.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A new owner loads snapshot + tail from the durable log and clients reconnect; no acked op is lost.
failureDrills.mitigation: Persist-before-ack + snapshot/tail recovery + doc router failover (with epoch fencing).

failureDrills.scenario: The server appends an op durably, then the ack is lost (client reconnects and retries the same op).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The retry carries the same clientOpId; the durable unique constraint rejects the duplicate and the server returns the original assigned version. The op is applied exactly once.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent appends keyed by (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id); ack returns serverVersion + clientOpId so the client reconciles its pending queue.

failureDrills.scenario: The persistence worker crashes while writing a snapshot.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The partial snapshot fails its checksum on load and is ignored; the reader falls back to the previous good snapshot plus a longer op-log tail. snapshot_version is never advanced for an uncommitted snapshot.
failureDrills.mitigation: Atomic snapshot write + checksum + format_version; advance documents.snapshot_version only after durable commit.

steps.title: 6. Presence: Cursors and Selections
description: Collaboration needs awareness: where others' cursors are, what they've selected, who's in the doc. Presence is high-frequency but ephemeral — it doesn't go in the durable op log.

Target design: presence is ephemeral in-memory state owned by the doc server itself, broadcast over the same WebSocket connection as edits, throttled (~10/s). There is no separate durable presence store on the hot path — the 'Presence' box in the architecture is that per-session in-memory cursor/selection state, not a standalone backend service. Cursors are transformed alongside ops (an insert before your cursor shifts it) so they stay anchored. On a failover, live cursor state is simply rebuilt as clients re-announce — it was never durable.

A dedicated pub/sub presence tier (the alternative option below) only earns its place if you need cross-document 'who is online' aggregation or presence fanout that outgrows the doc server; for the bounded-editors-per-doc case here, in-server ephemeral presence is enough.

Presence is best-effort and lossy: missing a cursor update for a moment is harmless, so it bypasses the durability path entirely. The one thing never to do is route cursor moves through the durable op log.

view.caption: The collab server updates its in-memory presence state with each cursor/selection change and streams other users' cursors back to the client on the same edit channel — no separate presence backend.

options.name: Ephemeral best-effort presence channel (default)
options.description: Cursor and selection state is tracked per editor and broadcast over the existing live connection, throttled and lossy, bypassing the durable op log entirely.
pros: Cursor/selection updates bypass the durable op log entirely, so they cost nothing on the persistence path.
High-frequency updates are throttled and lossy-tolerant — a dropped cursor frame is harmless.
Reuses the existing live connection, so no extra infrastructure for awareness.
cons: Presence is not durable: on a server failover, live cursor state is rebuilt from scratch.
Cursors must still be transformed alongside ops so they stay anchored as text shifts.
No history of where people were — fine for stories, but not auditable.

view.caption: Cursor and selection updates live in ephemeral best-effort in-memory state on the doc server, which pushes other users' cursors to the client without durability.

options.name: Presence through the durable op log
options.description: Cursor moves are written as ordinary ops into the op log, sequenced and broadcast on the same authoritative path as text edits.
pros: One uniform path: cursors are just another op, ordered and broadcast like edits.
Cursor positions stay consistent with the exact document version they reference.
No separate presence service to build or operate.
cons: Floods the op log with high-frequency, low-value cursor moves, bloating storage and replay.
Couples ephemeral awareness to durability — every cursor twitch incurs an fsync-class cost.
Slows the real edit stream behind a torrent of presence traffic.

view.caption: Presence is carried as ops through the durable operation log, so cursor updates are persisted and broadcast like edits.

options.name: Dedicated pub/sub presence service
options.description: A separate pub/sub tier handles cursor and selection traffic, aggregating awareness across many documents independently of the doc servers.
pros: A separate presence/pub-sub tier scales awareness independently of the doc servers.
Can aggregate presence across many documents and surface 'who is online' globally.
Keeps high-frequency cursor traffic off the collab server's hot path.
cons: Another service to deploy, scale, and keep in sync with session membership.
Cursors still need transforming against ops, so the doc server must feed it op context.
Overkill for the bounded editors-per-doc case the rest of the design assumes.

view.caption: The collab server publishes cursors to a dedicated presence pub/sub service that fans other users' cursors out to clients.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should cursor positions go through the same durable, ordered path as document edits?
whyNow: Presence is a core collaborative feature with a different (ephemeral, best-effort) consistency profile — recognizing that separation is the key insight.

concepts.definition: High-frequency, non-durable awareness state (cursors, selections) broadcast best-effort.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the durable op path clean and cheap while still feeling live.
concepts.example: Cursor updates throttled to ~10/s, never persisted.

recap.after: Live cursors/selections, anchored through transforms, broadcast cheaply as ephemeral in-server state.
recap.newRisk: Offline editing and reconnection still need to merge buffered ops correctly.

traps.trap: Persisting cursor/selection updates through the durable op log.
traps.why: Cursor moves are ~10/s/editor and ephemeral; logging them floods storage and replay and incurs an fsync-class cost per twitch, starving the real edit stream.
traps.instead: Keep presence as throttled, best-effort, in-memory state broadcast on the live connection; never durable.

steps.title: 7. Offline Editing and Reconnection
description: A client can lose connection and keep editing. It buffers ops locally (in the client-local pending-ops store, not a backend queue) against the last baseline it had, each tagged with a monotonic clientSeq and a durable clientOpId. On reconnect it runs a precise protocol rather than just 'send everything'.

Reconnect protocol: the client sends its lastSeenVersion and its pending ops. The server (1) returns the ops the client missed (baseline -> head) so the client can rebase its pending ops over them, then (2) accepts the pending ops idempotently — duplicates from a prior partial sync are deduped by their durable clientOpId, so an op that was actually applied before the disconnect is not applied again even if the client reconnected under a fresh session. This reuses the same OT machinery as online concurrent editing, just with a bigger gap.

Edge cases that matter in production: (a) a very stale client whose baseline is older than the retained op-log tail can't be caught up op-by-op — the server replies needsSnapshot and the client resyncs from a snapshot, then rebases its pending ops onto it; (b) the document was deleted or the client's permission was revoked while it was offline — on reconnect the server checks acl_version and rejects the buffered ops (permission-denied / doc-gone) rather than silently applying stale edits; (c) the snapshot the client's baseline referenced has been compacted away — same needsSnapshot path.

Because edits are idempotent operations against a versioned baseline, offline merge is the same problem as concurrent online editing — just with a bigger gap and an explicit catch-up/dedupe/resync step — so no separate merge mechanism is needed.

view.caption: The client buffers edits in its local (on-device) pending-ops store and replays them to the collab server on reconnect, which dedupes and appends them to the operation log.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user edits offline for an hour, then reconnects. How do their changes merge with everyone else's?
whyNow: Offline support tests whether the conflict model truly generalizes; reusing OT/CRDT for reconnection is the elegant payoff.

concepts.definition: On reconnect the client fetches missed ops, rebases its pending ops over them, and resubmits; the server dedupes by durable clientOpId.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns the scary 'offline for an hour' case into ordinary concurrent editing with an explicit, idempotent catch-up step.
concepts.example: Client at v412 reconnects to head v460; rebases pending seq 87 over 413..460, resubmits, server skips already-applied ops.

concepts.definition: If the client's baseline is older than the retained tail, the server sends needsSnapshot and the client rebases its pending ops onto a fresh snapshot.
concepts.whyItMatters: Op-by-op catch-up is impossible once the tail is compacted; snapshot resync bounds the worst case.
concepts.example: Offline for days; baseline v100 long compacted; resync from snapshot v455.

concepts.definition: Operate the system on user-visible editing signals: op-ack latency, remote-broadcast lag, pending ops per client, transform-failure rate, snapshot age, tail replay length, owner failover time, stale-epoch rejection rate, duplicate-op retry rate, presence drop/coalesce rate, reconnect success rate, and permission-denied buffered-op count.
concepts.whyItMatters: These map directly to how the editor feels and to the correctness invariants — a spike in stale-epoch rejections means a flapping owner; a spike in duplicate-op retries means clients aren't getting acks.
concepts.example: Alert when broadcast lag p99 > 100ms or owner failover time p95 > one lease TTL.

concepts.definition: The incidents worth a runbook here aren't DLQ replays — they're stale-owner fencing storms, snapshot checksum failures, hot-doc saturation, slow-client eviction, and stuck lease renewal.
concepts.whyItMatters: Each ties to a concrete signal and action: checksum failure -> fall back to prior snapshot + longer tail; stuck lease -> force-expire and re-grant; hot-doc saturation -> shed/queue presence first, then throttle ops.
concepts.example: Snapshot checksum fails on attach -> automatically load prior good snapshot + replay a longer tail, page if it recurs.

recap.after: Offline ops buffered locally and merged on reconnect via catch-up + idempotent dedupe; snapshot resync for very stale clients.
recap.newRisk: Very stale clients and large gaps — bounded by snapshot resync; hot docs stress one server.

traps.trap: Replaying buffered offline ops on reconnect without dedupe or a permission re-check.
traps.why: Ops that were already applied before the disconnect get applied twice (corruption), and edits buffered after a revoke get written despite the client no longer having access.
traps.instead: Dedupe by durable clientOpId, rebase over missed ops, and re-check acl_version before accepting buffered ops.

failureDrills.scenario: A client edits offline, then reconnects, and some of its buffered ops were actually delivered and applied before the disconnect (partial prior sync).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The server recognizes the already-applied clientOpId entries, skips them, and applies only the genuinely new pending ops — exactly once, no duplication.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent appends + reconnect catch-up that returns missed ops for client-side rebase.

failureDrills.scenario: A client's edit permission is revoked (or the doc is deleted) while it is offline.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On reconnect the server sees a stale acl_version and rejects the buffered ops with permission-denied / doc-gone instead of applying stale edits; the client surfaces the loss of access.
failureDrills.mitigation: acl_version checked on reconnect and on each op submission; buffered ops are not privileged over live ones.

flows.name: Reconnect after offline editing (catch-up, rebase, idempotent replay)
flows.note: The client sends its last seen version and pending ops; the server returns missed ops for the client to rebase over, then accepts the pending ops idempotently. If the baseline is too old, it asks for a snapshot resync instead.

bottlenecks.issue: A very popular doc concentrates load on one server.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Bounded editors per doc; the single-owner model is per-doc, so load spreads across docs. Batch keystrokes into ops to cut the per-doc op rate.

bottlenecks.issue: A client offline for a very long time.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Resync from a snapshot instead of replaying every missed op.

bottlenecks.issue: A slow client can't keep up with broadcast (backpressure).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Cap pending unacked ops per client and per-connection send buffer; coalesce/drop redundant presence frames; if a client falls too far behind, force a snapshot resync rather than queueing unboundedly.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Real-Time Collaborative Editor
finalDesign.description: Target: a single owning doc server per document + Operational Transformation + a durable op log. Clients apply edits optimistically and stream idempotent ops (clientId, sessionId, clientOpId, clientSeq, baseVersion) to the one collab server that owns the document, reached via a doc router/sharding under a fenced ownership lease. That server assigns a total order, transforms concurrent ops (OT), appends them to a durable op log (persist-before-ack, deduped by a durable (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id) key that survives reload/restart/offline gaps), and broadcasts the ordered ops so all clients converge by replaying the accepted set. Snapshots plus the op-log tail bound load and enable crash/failover recovery; ownership transfer bumps an owner_epoch and fences stale owners so there is never a second writer. Presence (cursors/selections) is ephemeral best-effort state on the doc server over the same connection. Offline edits buffer client-locally and merge on reconnect through the same OT machinery with idempotent dedupe, falling back to snapshot resync for long gaps. CRDT is the alternative we'd choose if the product shifted to offline-first/P2P. Invariants: exactly one active owner epoch per doc, durable append before ack, idempotent client op IDs, clients converge by replaying the accepted op set under the server order, presence is best-effort.

view.caption: The editor client connects via the gateway and document router to the owning collab server, which orders ops into the log, broadcasts edits, tracks presence in memory, snapshots via the persistence worker, and replays the client-local pending-ops buffer on reconnect.

functional.how: Optimistic local apply + single-server ordering + transform.
Server broadcasts ordered ops over live connections.
OT/CRDT reconciliation over a single op order.
Offline buffer + merge; op log + snapshots for history.

nonFunctional.how: OT/CRDT over a single authoritative order.
Snapshot + tail reload on failover, with epoch fencing so only the new owner writes.
Shard by doc id: one owning server per active doc, bounded editors per doc, so load spreads horizontally across documents.

nonFunctional.requirement: Available: brief server blips shouldn't lose work
nonFunctional.how: Persist-before-ack + fenced failover reload, plus client-local offline buffering and idempotent replay so a blip is just a reconnect.

tradeoff: Self-hosting a sticky WebSocket fleet gives full control over routing to the owning doc server but you operate connection draining, autoscaling, and reconnection yourself.
Managed gateways remove that ops burden but can constrain sticky routing and message-size/duration limits that matter for doc sessions.
makesIrrelevant: A managed pub/sub gateway can absorb the presence fanout tier, removing the need for a separate presence service.

tradeoff: A log/stream system gives natural append-only ordering and replay but needs a uniqueness mechanism for idempotent (session, seq) dedupe layered on top.
A transactional DB table makes the (doc_id, client_id, client_op_id) unique constraint and monotonic (doc_id, version) trivial, at the cost of per-doc write throughput tuning.
Object storage is cheap and durable for periodic snapshots and archived history; it is not low-latency for the hot op path, which is why the op log stays separate.
A coordination store (etcd/ZooKeeper) gives strong lease/fencing primitives for owner_epoch but is one more system to run.
A managed strongly-consistent DB can hold ownership + ACLs + lease TTL together, simplifying ops at the cost of less specialized fencing semantics.
makesIrrelevant: A lease store with built-in fencing tokens removes the need to hand-roll split-brain detection in the router.

tradeoff: Adopting a mature OT/CRDT library avoids the notoriously hard correctness work of transform functions or tombstone GC, but ties the op/document model to that library's representation.
Rolling your own gives full control over the op schema (important for rich text) but you own every transform-correctness edge case.

Blast radius: a managed gateway/relay or hosted realtime DB lowers ops burden but a regional outage takes editing down globally and stream-retention limits cap your replay window; self-hosting spreads blast radius but you carry the on-call.
Self-hosted metrics/tracing give full cardinality (per-doc, per-owner) for hot-doc debugging but you run the storage and alerting yourself.
Managed observability is turnkey but high-cardinality per-doc metrics get expensive — sample or aggregate to per-owner/per-shard for the firehose signals.
makesIrrelevant: Tracking duplicate-op retry rate and stale-epoch rejection rate as first-class metrics removes the need to reconstruct split-brain or double-apply incidents from raw logs after the fact.

say: Confirm real-time multi-user editing with guaranteed convergence.
Pin instant local feedback and durability of acked edits.
Note bounded editors per doc — correctness is the hard part, not scale.
Optimistic local apply; ops streamed to the server.
One owner server per doc assigns a total order via an op log.
Clients replay the ordered ops to converge.
Conflict resolution: OT (target) vs CRDT and the tradeoff.
Idempotent ops (durable clientOpId) and owner-epoch fencing for safe retries and failover.
Snapshots + op log for fast load and crash recovery.
Presence as ephemeral state; offline buffering, catch-up, and dedupe on reconnect.
Tradeoffs: OT (the target) vs CRDT, snapshot frequency, single-owner failover with epoch fencing.
Name the correctness guards: idempotent client op IDs, persist-before-ack, owner-epoch fencing.
Mention observability (ack latency, broadcast lag, duplicate-op rate, failover time), backpressure, rich content, and access control as extensions.

expectations: Streams edits to a server and broadcasts.
May not articulate OT/CRDT or durability.
Reasons about OT transform correctness vs CRDT metadata cost and commits to OT for this case.
Designs failover from snapshot+tail with owner-epoch fencing to prevent split brain.
Specifies idempotent ops (durable clientOpId dedupe surviving reload/restart), persist-before-ack, and backpressure limits.
Discusses cursor transformation and long-offline resync (catch-up, rebase, snapshot resync) precisely.
Names observability signals and per-op authorization / ACL revocation handling.

followUps: OT vs CRDT — which would you choose for a server-centric editor vs an offline-first one, and why?
How do you transform a cursor position when concurrent edits land before it?
How do you fail over a document's owning server without losing in-flight ops?
How would you support rich text (formatting), not just plain characters?
How do you resync a client that's been offline for days?

What metrics and alerts would you watch — op-ack latency, broadcast lag, pending ops per client, transform failures, snapshot age, tail replay length, failover time, duplicate-op rate, presence fanout?
How do you authorize each op (not just the connect), and handle a permission revocation mid-session?
